If you are the chief executive officer and controlling shareholder=C2=A0of =
two different companies, each with different minority shareholders and empl=
oyees, and they both use the same essential scarce inputs, and the sole sup=
plier of those inputs calls you up and says =E2=80=9Cwe are backed up and=
=C2=A0can only meet the needs of one of your companies, which one should ge=
t the inputs and which should go without,=E2=80=9D what should you do?The e=
xtremely obvious answer is that you should not be the CEO and controlling s=
hareholder of two different companies that compete for the same inputs! The=
re is not a=C2=A0good=C2=A0answer! You can=E2=80=99t, like, put this proble=
m into the=C2=A0Good Governance Machine and have it come out clean.=C2=A0Th=
e problem is that you have a fiduciary obligation to the shareholders of on=
e company to put their interests first, and you have a fiduciary obligation=
 to the shareholders of the other company to put their interests first, and=
 you cannot do both. This is why one person is not usually the CEO of two d=
ifferent companies that compete with each other, and, when someone is, peop=
le get mad at him all the time.Elon Musk is the CEO and controlling shareho=
lder of like six different companies,
  [1]
=C2=A0many=C2=A0of which are to varying degrees in the artificial intellige=
nce business, so=C2=A0of course=C2=A0everyone is mad at him all the time. C=

d call options, (2) posting weird stuff online that makes people buy the st=

eptive intent, and there doesn=E2=80=99t seem to be any evidence of that. H=
is weird online posts don=E2=80=99t contain any=C2=A0lies, in part because =
almost none of them contain any factual assertions at all; they=E2=80=99re =
just movie clips and cartoons and stuff. (One Reddit post lists his purport=
ed ownership of GameStop, which is=C2=A0definitely=C2=A0a factual assertion=

e were posting, causing GameStop to rally, and then=C2=A0dumping=C2=A0his s=
tock, that=C2=A0might look like a sort of deception, but there=E2=80=99s no=
 evidence of that either; as far as I can tell, he likes the stock and keep=
s plowing his profits back into it.
  [5]
So if you asked me=C2=A0whether Roaring Kitty is=C2=A0breaking any rules wi=
th his GameStop posting and trading, I would say =E2=80=9Cprobably not.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0Further evidence could change my mind, but right now it looks f=
ine.But I am just a columnist on the internet, that is not legal advice, an=
d I have absolutely no skin in the game.
  [6]
=C2=A0You could ask other people, who might have more constraints on their =
answers. For instance,=C2=A0the US Securities and Exchange Commission has a=
n interest in preventing market manipulation, and I expect it has pondered =
Gill=E2=80=99s actions. But the SEC has only two possible answers to this q=
uestion
  [7]
:=09=E2=80=9CWe think=C2=A0we can, with a high degree of likelihood, prove =
in court that this is market manipulation,=E2=80=9D or=09=E2=80=9CWe do not=
 think that we can prove in court that this is market manipulation.=E2=80=
=9DAnswer 2 encompasses both =E2=80=9Cthis is entirely innocent and in fact=
 we love it=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cwe hate this and think it=E2=80=99s clear=
ly illegal, but=C2=A0the risk of losing at trial is too high.=E2=80=9D The =
SEC=E2=80=99s bar for=C2=A0actually bringing a case=C2=A0is pretty high, an=
d it probably lets slide some amount of stuff that it thinks is bad.Morgan =
Stanley=E2=80=99s set of possible answers is different, though. The Wall St=
reet Journal reports:E*Trade is considering telling meme-stock leader Keith=
 Gill he can no longer use its platform after growing concerned about poten=
tial stock manipulation around his recent purchases of GameStop options, ac=
cording to people familiar with the matter. =E2=80=A6At E*Trade and its own=
er Morgan Stanley, [Gill=E2=80=99s]=C2=A0power created concerns he can pump=
 up a stock for his own benefit. Their debate includes whether his actions =
amounted to manipulation and whether or not the firm is willing to risk dra=
wing the attention of his meme army by removing him, according to people fa=
miliar with their internal discussions. ...The Securities and Exchange Comm=
ission has also been reviewing trading in GameStop call options around the =
time of Gill=E2=80=99s social-media posts, according to people familiar wit=
h the agency=E2=80=99s efforts. The options trades sparked internal discuss=
ions at the SEC over whether they could be considered manipulation, one of =
these people said. It couldn=E2=80=99t be determined whether the SEC was re=
viewing Gill specifically. =E2=80=A6Morgan Stanley employees =E2=80=A6=C2=
=A0saw he had purchased call options before [Gill=E2=80=99s May 12] tweet, =
the people said. A call option gives a trader the right to buy the stock by=
 a certain date at a stated price. At least some of those options expired t=
hat week, one of the people said. That meant Gill=E2=80=99s trades likely g=
enerated profits thanks to the stock move his tweet generated.=C2=A0Morgan =
Stanley=E2=80=99s global financial-crimes unit and external counsel began d=
iscussing whether Gill=E2=80=99s actions were legal and whether the firm sh=
ould cancel his E*Trade account, the people said.Ordinarily, if you are Mor=
gan Stanley=C2=A0and your global financial-crimes unit is having discussion=
s with outside counsel about whether a=C2=A0retail client=E2=80=99s trades =
are illegal, you have a very strong incentive to cancel his account. You ca=

=80=99re not sure that he=E2=80=99s manipulating the market, why take the r=
isk? If it turns out that he is,=C2=A0then=C2=A0you=C2=A0will look bad. The=
 SEC might say that you saw the red flags in his account and didn=E2=80=99t=

e retail trader; a big one, sure, but he=E2=80=99s probably not generating =

=C2=A0What is the incentive to keep him around?That is, ordinarily, the SEC=

Last week, the US stock market transitioned from T+2 to T+1 settlement. Bef=
ore, if you bought stock on the stock exchange on Monday, you actually got =
the shares and delivered the cash=C2=A0on Wednesday. Now, if you buy stock =
on the stock exchange on Monday, you get the shares and deliver the cash on=
 Tuesday. There are various processes that have to happen between trade and=
 settlement (finding or recalling stock borrow, converting foreign currenci=
es, etc.), and people were nervous that something would break when the sett=
lement time was shortened, but so far things seem to have worked fine. Ther=
e has even been talk of eventually moving to a shorter T+0 settlement cycle=
, where trades settle later the same day.That said, here=E2=80=99s one more=
 process that sometimes has to happen between trading and settlement:A glit=
ch during a software update early Monday led the New York Stock Exchange to=
 erroneously halt trading on about 40 stocks and display odd trades showing=
 a 99% drop in companies including Warren Buffett=E2=80=99s Berkshire Hatha=
way Inc.The disruption =E2=80=94 the third episode to hit US markets in the=
 past week =E2=80=94 was resolved after roughly 45 minutes when the Consoli=
dated Tape Association, whose systems are operated by a NYSE subsidiary, re=
verted to a backup data center running a different software version. ...Abo=
ut a dozen trades in Berkshire Class A shares went off at $185.10 around 9:=
50 a.m. The stock closed Friday at $627,400. NYSE said any trade between 9:=
50 and 9:51 at or below $603,718.30 will be canceled. NuScale Power Corp. h=
ad a similar glitch, with trades that printed at about 99% below the prior =
price.Every once in a while, a stock exchange will print a bunch of trades =
that are =E2=80=9Cclearly erroneous,=E2=80=9D and will then come to its sen=
ses, decide that the trades were wrong, and cancel them. If you cancel trad=
es between trade and settlement, that can be a mess =E2=80=94=C2=A0what hap=
pens to people who bought Berkshire at $185.10 and immediately sold at $604=
,000? =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is more or less possible. If you try to cancel =
them after the money has moved, though, that=E2=80=99s harder. A shorter se=
ttlement window might make these glitches more dangerous.Of course, it is s=
ort of dumb to have the glitches in the first place. If a stock exchange ha=
s criteria like =E2=80=9Ca trade that is more than X% below the last trade =
is clearly erroneous and should be canceled,=E2=80=9D then it should probab=
ly code that rule into its matching engine and=C2=A0prevent the trades from=
 happening, rather than coding the rule into its rule book and canceling th=
em after the fact. I suppose the less time there is between trade and settl=
ement, the more reason there is to do that.

   =20
 =20
I wrote yesterday about =E2=80=9Csynthetic PIK=E2=80=9D private credit deal=
s. In a PIK (payment in kind) loan, a company borrows money and can pay int=
erest in the form of more debt: You borrow $100 with an interest rate of 10=
%; you don=E2=80=99t pay cash interest, but after the first year, you owe $=
110, then $121, etc.
  [9]
=C2=A0In a synthetic PIK loan, the company borrows money and pays interest =
in=C2=A0cash, but it=C2=A0gets=C2=A0the cash by=C2=A0borrowing=C2=A0it from=
 the lender: You borrow $100, in the first year you pay $10 cash interest, =
you borrow the $10 from the same lender, so at the end of the first year yo=
u owe $110, etc. The interest payments are funded with a delayed-draw term =
loan: You sign the extra loan at the same time as the regular loan, and the=
n draw on it over time to make the interest payments.Why? Why go through th=
e circularity of borrowing more money to pay cash, instead of just adding i=
t to the tab? One rationale is that the private credit funds that make thes=
e loans get some of their financing from banks, and=C2=A0the banks limit th=
e amount of PIK debt they can do (because it=E2=80=99s risky), so this is a=
 way around that. I wrote:The private credit fund doesn=E2=80=99t care: Pay=
ing interest in the form of more debt, or paying interest in cash while sim=
ultaneously borrowing more, are exactly identical transactions as far as it=
s credit risk is concerned. What matters is that the private credit fund ha=
s some contract with somebody else that says =E2=80=9Cwe promise not to do =
too many PIK deals,=E2=80=9D and a synthetic PIK deal is not technically a =
PIK deal. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The somebody else, specifically, is the banks.Well=
, a=C2=A0reader emailed to point out that there=E2=80=99s another somebody =
else:Most private credit funds collect management fees based on invested LP=
 [limited partner]=C2=A0capital. Traditionally, if an investment was accrui=
ng PIK interest, they=E2=80=99d only get management fees paid on the initia=
l principal amount funded with LP capital and not the accreted PIK portion.=
 Now that they are funding it with DDTL [a=C2=A0delayed draw term loan], th=
ey get to collect a management fee on that piece that is funded to pay the =
interest. So, the LPs are paying a management fee for as long as the DDTL d=
rawn loan is outstanding (perhaps a couple of years?) to pay themselves one=
 quarter=E2=80=99s worth of interest and are concurrently increasing their =
invested capital exposure to that specific deal.If a private credit fund=C2=
=A0does a $100 PIK loan with 10% interest, after a year the borrower owes $=
110 and the fund can charge fees on=C2=A0$100 of capital. If it does the sa=
me loan as a synthetic PIK, after a year=C2=A0the borrower owes $110 and th=
e fund can charge fees on=C2=A0$110 of capital.=C2=A0

e 13% of Tesla Inc.=E2=80=99s stock, though he is probably a =E2=80=9Ccontr=
olling shareholder=E2=80=9D there as a legal matter.=20


  [2] This requires X to have a board, with independent directors represent=
ing the minority shareholders.


  [3] Bloomberg=20
notes that "Musk obliquely confirmed CNBC=E2=80=99s reporting on redirectin=
g Nvidia=E2=80=99s GPUs to X and xAI, writing on his social media service T=
uesday that 'Tesla had no place to send the Nvidia chips to turn them on, s=
o they would have just sat in a warehouse.'" Maybe the committees would hav=
e reached the same result.


  [4] I=E2=80=99m exaggerating a little here, because CNBC goes on to note =
that the chips going to X might *really* be going to xAI, Musk=E2=80=99s *s=
eparate* company for doing general AI stuff: =E2=80=9CX and xAI are tightly=
 intertwined. In a post on X in November, Musk wrote, =E2=80=98X Corp inves=
tors will own 25% of xAI.=E2=80=99 Additionally, xAI uses some capacity in =
X data centers to run some of its training and inference for the large lang=
uage models behind its chatbot, called Grok, CNBC has learned.=E2=80=9D


  [5] As I=20
pointed out yesterday, he owns a ton of GameStop call options that expire o=
n June 21; he *can=E2=80=99t* hold those indefinitely. But it does seem as =
if his trading strategy has involved buying short-dated call options, selli=
ng them at a profit and rolling the profits into more call options, which i=
s perhaps not quite a =E2=80=9Cbuy and hold=E2=80=9D strategy, but which is=
 certainly not a =E2=80=9Cpump and dump=E2=80=9D either. Also, I asked yest=
erday where Gill got the money to buy something like $175 million worth of =
GameStop stock and options, and the consensus seems to be that he made a lo=
t of money trading GameStop options last month and rolled his profits into =
his current position. (Luke Kawa writes that =E2=80=9Cdoing some quick math=
 shows how difficult it is to ascribe of all of those would-be gains to las=
t month=E2=80=99s frenzy in shares of GameStop,=E2=80=9D but maybe you can =
get there.)


  [6] Except, I suppose, that as a columnist on the internet, more nonsense=
 is always good for me.


  [7] I=E2=80=99m being a little loose, because *right now* the SEC=E2=80=
=99s answer is presumably =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re looking into it.=E2=80=9D =
The SEC has a fairly leisurely amount of time to ponder the question, and c=
an do some investigating, before deciding one way or another.


  [8] E*Trade charges fees of 50 or 65 cents per options contract. Also of =
course it makes money on payment for order flow. I suppose if I were E*Trad=
e I=E2=80=99d be a *little* tempted to sell Keith Gill=E2=80=99s order flow=
 separately from the rest of the retail flow. People would pay a *lot* of m=
oney for Keith Gill=E2=80=99s order flow! Probably there's some legal probl=
em with doing that though.


  [9] Or: You can pay 10% cash interest, or choose to instead capitalize 11=
% =E2=80=94 often the PIK rate is higher than the cash rate.=20

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

5px;">Oh Elon</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are the chief executive officer=
 and controlling shareholder=C2=A0of two different companies, each with dif=
ferent minority shareholders and employees, and they both use the same esse=
ntial scarce inputs, and the sole supplier of those inputs calls you up and=
 says =E2=80=9Cwe are backed up and=C2=A0can only meet the needs of one of =
your companies, which one should get the inputs and which should go without=
,=E2=80=9D what should you do?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The extremel=
y obvious answer is that you should not be the CEO and controlling sharehol=
der of two different companies that compete for the same inputs! There is n=
ot a=C2=A0<em>good=C2=A0</em>answer! You can=E2=80=99t, like, put this prob=
lem into the=C2=A0Good Governance Machine and have it come out clean.=C2=A0=
The problem is that you have a fiduciary obligation to the shareholders of =
one company to put their interests first, and you have a fiduciary obligati=
on to the shareholders of the other company to put their interests first, a=
nd you cannot do both. This is why one person is not usually the CEO of two=
 different companies that compete with each other, and, when someone is, pe=
ople get mad at him all the time.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elon Musk=
 is the CEO and controlling shareholder of like six different companies,<a =
class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transpar=
ent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0many=C2=A0of which are to varying degrees in the artificial intel=
ligence business, so=C2=A0<em>of course=C2=A0</em>everyone is mad at him al=
l the time. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/356027=
04.261315/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY25iYy5jb20vMjAyNC8wNi8wNC9lbG9uLW11c2stdG9sZC1ud=
mlkaWEtdG8tc2hpcC1haS1jaGlwcy1yZXNlcnZlZC1mb3ItdGVzbGEtdG8teC14YWkuaHRtbA/6=
07f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1580798f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"items=
cope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !imp=
ortant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bott=
om-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">CNBC reports today</a>:</p><block=
quote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elon Musk claims he can grow Tesla into =
=E2=80=9Ca leader in AI & robotics,=E2=80=9D an ambition that he=E2=80=
=99s said will require a lot of pricey processors from Nvidia to build up i=
ts infrastructure.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On Tesla=E2=80=99s first=
-quarter earnings call in April, Musk said the electric vehicle company wil=
l increase the number of active H100s =E2=80=94 Nvidia=E2=80=99s flagship a=
rtificial intelligence chip =E2=80=94 from 35,000 to 85,000 by the end of t=
his year. He also wrote in a post on X a few days later that Tesla would sp=
end $10 billion this year =E2=80=9Cin combined training and inference AI.=
=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But emails written by Nvidia seni=
or staff and widely shared inside the company suggest that Musk presented a=
n exaggerated picture of Tesla=E2=80=99s procurement to shareholders. Corre=
spondence from Nvidia staffers also indicates that Musk diverted a sizable =
shipment of AI processors that had been reserved for Tesla to his social me=
dia company X, formerly known as Twitter.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">T=
esla shares slipped as much as 1% on the news Tuesday morning.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By ordering Nvidia to let privately held X jump the li=
ne ahead of Tesla, Musk pushed back the automaker=E2=80=99s receipt of more=
 than $500 million in graphics processing units, or GPUs, by months, likely=
 adding to delays in setting up the supercomputers Tesla says it needs to d=
evelop autonomous vehicles and humanoid robots.</p></blockquote><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">I love the timing here: Musk is <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35602704.261315/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=
nLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDQtMTcvZWxvbi13YW50cy1oaXMtbW9uZXktYm=
Fjaz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA0MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY=
2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA2MDQmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cBed5a2ff4" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">asking Tesla=E2=80=99s shareholders =
to vote</a>,=C2=A0<em>next week</em>, to approve an 11-figure compensation =
package for him for all the good work he has done running Tesla.</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Do you think this story helps him or hurts him? Like,=
 on the one hand, if I were a Tesla shareholder, I might be kind of mad abo=
ut this, and be less inclined to give him his money.</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">On the other hand, the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/35602704.261315/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9u=
L2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMTYvbW9yZ2FuLXN0YW5sZXktbGVha2VkLXNvbWUtYmxvY2stdHJ=
hZGVzP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA2MDQyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdX=
JjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDYwNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe96fea26" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">more or less explicit rationale</a=
> for giving him the money is =E2=80=9Cif you don=E2=80=99t give him the mo=
ney, he=E2=80=99s going to focus less on=C2=A0Tesla and prioritize his othe=
r companies instead.=E2=80=9D I suppose that, last week, Tesla shareholders=
=C2=A0might have found that threat somewhat vague: How much damage could he=
 really do by spending a bit less time on Tesla? Might his attention=C2=A0b=
e=C2=A0<em>bad=C2=A0</em>for Tesla, a mature car company, at this point? Mi=
ght it do=C2=A0<em>better=C2=A0</em>with less inspired but more normal mana=
gement? And now the threat is a bit more concrete: It=E2=80=99s=C2=A0=E2=80=
=9Csay goodbye to your AI chips if Musk loses interest.=E2=80=9D</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By the way, I=E2=80=99m sure someone has some ideas f=
or the Good Governance Machine that could make this work. Like: You set up =
a special committee of independent Tesla directors,=C2=A0you set up a speci=
al committee of independent X directors,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D=
"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-=
block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0the two committees get in a room together with Musk,=C2=A0he says=
 =E2=80=9Chey there are this many chips, you guys work out who gets them,=
=E2=80=9D he leaves, the committees each meet and figure out how much they =
want the chips, then they negotiate and cut a deal=C2=A0(whoever wants them=
 more pays the other company for priority,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> or they share, or Tesla gets the chips in exchange for training X=E2=
=80=99s large language models on parked Teslas, or whatever), and then they=
 go back to Musk with the deal and he approves whatever they come up with. =
There is a widespread sense that you can wash any sort of governance proble=
m through special committees, though people tend to believe that=C2=A0<em>l=
ess=C2=A0</em>about Elon Musk companies.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Al=
so this process sort of doesn=E2=80=99t make sense here, or with Elon Musk =
generally. He=E2=80=99s a hard-charging visionary entrepreneur! His=C2=A0<e=
m>thing </em>is making bold decisions about what to prioritize! Here, he ha=
s decided that his website for complaining on the internet needs artificial=
 intelligence capabilities more urgently than his self-driving car company =
does.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: =
transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0He=E2=80=99s not going to delegate=C2=A0a momentous decision like=
 that to a committee of independent directors; that=E2=80=99s the sort of d=
ecisive action under real-world constraints that people want to put in the =
hands of Elon Musk. It=E2=80=99s just that he=E2=80=99s not deciding betwee=
n two=C2=A0<em>projects=C2=A0</em>or=C2=A0<em>divisions=C2=A0</em>at one co=
mpany, where everyone benefits from his decision. He=E2=80=99s deciding bet=
ween two companies, and one of them will lose out.</p><table class=3D"livei=
ntent-sponsored-content" width=3D"auto" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0"=
>

t beneficiary of OpenAI=E2=80=99s success. The holdings he controls were wo=
rth at least $2.8 billion as of early this year, according to company filin=
gs and Wall Street Journal reporting. Much of the portfolio isn=E2=80=99t w=
idely known. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A growing number of Altman=

major business partners. The arrangement puts Altman on both sides of deals=

;">Last month, OpenAI announced a partnership with Reddit in which it would=

14. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Altman=E2=80=99s more recent =

ial intelligence boom being driven by OpenAI. Apex Security, in which Altma=
n invested an undisclosed amount last summer, aims to sell cybersecurity so=
ftware to companies using AI products such as ChatGPT. He also invested an =
undisclosed amount in Exowatt, a startup tackling the clean-energy needs of=
 big data centers used by AI companies.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">Part of the answer is the usual special committee stuff: Altman r=
ecuses himself from negotiations between his companies, he discloses everyt=
hing to the OpenAI board of directors, etc. That=E2=80=99s probably true: I=
t seems like the value to Altman here comes more from spotting the opportun=
ities to work with OpenAI than it does from detailed price negotiations. He=

ion valuation</a>; Altman is rich. But maybe =E2=80=9Cour CEO will work for=

 <em>is</em> the right way to structure that sort of pseudo-nonprofit? Mayb=

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Is Keith Gill, the meme-stock influenc=
er known as Roaring Kitty, doing market manipulation? He does seem to be=C2=
=A0(1) buying GameStop Corp. stock and call options, (2) <a href=3D"https:/=
/link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35602704.261315/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxv=
b21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDUtMTMvZ2FtZXN0b3AtaXMtYmFjaz9=
jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA0MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bm=
V3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA2MDQmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB7fcf47cb" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=

border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;">posting weird stuff online</a> that makes=
 people buy the stock and (3) <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/35602704.261315/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2F=
ydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDYtMDMvZ2FtZXN0b3AtY29tZXMtcm9hcmluZy1iYWNrP2NtcGlkPUJCRD=

nV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDYwNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a=
23cB482f1160" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bla=
nk" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;">profiting</a>. Those are things you would do if you w=
ere manipulating GameStop stock.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the oth=
er hand, manipulation requires some sort of deceptive intent, and there doe=
sn=E2=80=99t seem to be any evidence of that. His weird online posts don=E2=
=80=99t contain any=C2=A0<em>lies</em>, in part because almost none of them=
 contain any factual assertions at all; they=E2=80=99re just movie clips an=
d cartoons and stuff. (One <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/35602704.261315/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cucmVkZGl0LmNvbS9yL1N1cGVyc3RvbmsvY2=
9tbWVudHMvMWQ2cjV2cC9nbWVfeW9sb191cGRhdGVfanVuZV8yXzIwMjQv/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cB0819a415" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;">Reddit post</a> lists his purported ownership of=
 GameStop, which is=C2=A0<em>definitely=C2=A0</em>a factual assertion and c=
learly moved the stock, but I assume it is true.) I suppose that if he were=
 posting, causing GameStop to rally, and then=C2=A0<em>dumping=C2=A0</em>hi=
s stock, that=C2=A0<em>might </em>look like a sort of deception, but there=
=E2=80=99s no evidence of that either; as far as I can tell, he likes the s=
tock and keeps plowing his profits back into it.<a class=3D"footnote-index"=
 href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display:=
 inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a></p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So if you asked <em>me</em>=C2=A0wheth=
er Roaring Kitty is=C2=A0breaking any rules with his GameStop posting and t=
rading, I would say =E2=80=9Cprobably not.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Further evidence c=
ould change my mind, but right now it looks fine.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">But I am just a columnist on the internet, that is not legal advice,=
 and I have absolutely no skin in the game.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a>=C2=A0You could ask other people, who might have more constraints on th=
eir answers. For instance,=C2=A0the US Securities and Exchange Commission h=
as an interest in preventing market manipulation, and I expect it has ponde=
red Gill=E2=80=99s actions. But the SEC has only two possible answers to th=
is question<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-c=
olor: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a>:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin:=
 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CWe think=C2=A0we ca=
n, with a high degree of likelihood, prove in court that this is market man=
ipulation,=E2=80=9D or</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CWe=
 do not think that we can prove in court that this is market manipulation.=
=E2=80=9D</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Answer 2 encompasses both =
=E2=80=9Cthis is entirely innocent and in fact we love it=E2=80=9D and =E2=
=80=9Cwe hate this and think it=E2=80=99s clearly illegal, but=C2=A0the ris=
k of losing at trial is too high.=E2=80=9D The SEC=E2=80=99s bar for=C2=A0<=
em>actually bringing a case=C2=A0</em>is pretty high, and it probably lets =
slide some amount of stuff that it thinks is bad.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">Morgan Stanley=E2=80=99s set of possible answers is different, thoug=
h. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35602704.261315=
/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9maW5hbmNlL3JlZ3VsYXRpb24vZS10cmFkZS1jb25zaWRlcn=
Mta2lja2luZy1tZW1lLXN0b2NrLWxlYWRlci1rZWl0aC1naWxsLW9mZi1wbGF0Zm9ybS1mMjAwM=
2VjND9tb2Q9aHBfbGVhZF9wb3Mx/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB00082ee8" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">The =
Wall Street Journal reports</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">E*Trade is considering telling meme-stock leader Keith Gill he can no lon=
ger use its platform after growing concerned about potential stock manipula=
tion around his recent purchases of GameStop options, according to people f=
amiliar with the matter. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At E*Tra=
de and its owner Morgan Stanley, [Gill=E2=80=99s]=C2=A0power created concer=
ns he can pump up a stock for his own benefit. Their debate includes whethe=
r his actions amounted to manipulation and whether or not the firm is willi=
ng to risk drawing the attention of his meme army by removing him, accordin=
g to people familiar with their internal discussions. ...</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">The Securities and Exchange Commission has also been reviewi=
ng trading in GameStop call options around the time of Gill=E2=80=99s socia=
l-media posts, according to people familiar with the agency=E2=80=99s effor=
ts. The options trades sparked internal discussions at the SEC over whether=
 they could be considered manipulation, one of these people said. It couldn=
=E2=80=99t be determined whether the SEC was reviewing Gill specifically. =
=E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Morgan Stanley employees =E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0saw he had purchased call options before [Gill=E2=80=99s May 12] t=
weet, the people said. A call option gives a trader the right to buy the st=
ock by a certain date at a stated price. At least some of those options exp=
ired that week, one of the people said. That meant Gill=E2=80=99s trades li=
kely generated profits thanks to the stock move his tweet generated.=C2=A0<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Morgan Stanley=E2=80=99s global financial-c=
rimes unit and external counsel began discussing whether Gill=E2=80=99s act=
ions were legal and whether the firm should cancel his E*Trade account, the=
 people said.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><em>Ordinarily</=
em>, if you are Morgan Stanley=C2=A0and your global financial-crimes unit i=
s having discussions with outside counsel about whether a=C2=A0<em>retail c=
lient=E2=80=99s trades are illegal</em>, you have a very strong incentive t=
o cancel his account. You can just do that! You=E2=80=99re allowed to close=
 his account! Even if you=E2=80=99re not <em>sure</em> that he=E2=80=99s ma=
nipulating the market, why take the risk? If it turns out that he is,=C2=A0=
then=C2=A0<em>you=C2=A0</em>will look bad. The SEC might say that you saw t=
he red flags in his account and didn=E2=80=99t do anything about them, and =
you might get in trouble. Meanwhile this is one retail trader; a big one, s=
ure, but he=E2=80=99s probably not generating all <em>that </em>much revenu=
e for you by making big zero-commission trades.<a class=3D"footnote-index" =
href=3D"#footnote-8" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: =
inline-block;">
<span>[8]</span>
</a>=C2=A0What is the incentive to keep him around?</p><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">That is, ordinarily, the SEC is the last line of defense against e=
gregious cases, but the brokers are the first line of defense against borde=
rline cases. If you look=C2=A0<em>kind of=C2=A0</em>like you=C2=A0<em>might=
=C2=A0</em>be doing market manipulation, the SEC might not bring a case, bu=
t it might hope that your broker will quietly shut you down.</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">Here, of course, the calculation is different:</p><blockq=
uote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some Morgan Stanley employees have been c=
oncerned that closing Gill=E2=80=99s account could result in the firm losin=
g E*Trade clients, the people said. Gill=E2=80=99s messages have been able =
to command a large following, one reason GameStop and other meme stocks hav=
e soared. Morgan Stanley employees have worried what would happen if Gill p=
osted on social media that he had been ditched by E*Trade and whether that =
might prompt many people to shut down their E*Trade accounts in solidarity,=
 the people said.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He=E2=80=99s=
 not=C2=A0<em>really=C2=A0</em>just one retail trader; he=E2=80=99s the voi=
ce of a movement, and if E*Trade shut him down they might lose a lot of cus=
tomers. So they need a really good reason to act.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">I suspect that's true of the SEC, too, by the way. Gill=E2=80=99s po=
sting and trading are not=C2=A0just ambiguous; they are also extremely popu=
lar and politically charged. If the SEC tries to shut him down, there are g=
oing to be angry letters and tweets and congressional hearings. If it=E2=80=
=99s going to stop this, the SEC had better be sure that it's right.</p><ta=
ble class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellpadd=
ing=3D"0">

, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Down 99%</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Last week, the US stock market transit=
ioned from T+2 to T+1 settlement. Before, if you bought stock on the stock =
exchange on Monday, you actually got the shares and delivered the cash=C2=
=A0on Wednesday. Now, if you buy stock on the stock exchange on Monday, you=
 get the shares and deliver the cash on Tuesday. There are various <a href=

any borrows money and can pay interest in the form of more debt: You borrow=
 $100 with an interest rate of 10%; you don=E2=80=99t pay cash interest, bu=
t after the first year, you owe $110, then $121, etc.<a class=3D"footnote-i=

<span>[9]</span>
</a>=C2=A0In a synthetic PIK loan, the company borrows money and pays inter=
est in=C2=A0<em>cash</em>, but it=C2=A0<em>gets=C2=A0</em>the cash by=C2=A0=
<em>borrowing=C2=A0</em>it from the lender: You borrow $100, in the first y=
ear you pay $10 cash interest, you borrow the $10 from the same lender, so =
at the end of the first year you owe $110, etc. The interest payments are f=
unded with a delayed-draw term loan: You sign the extra loan at the same ti=
me as the regular loan, and then draw on it over time to make the interest =
payments.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Why? Why go through the circulari=
ty of borrowing more money to pay cash, instead of just adding it to the ta=
b? One rationale is that the private credit funds that make these loans get=
 some of their financing from banks, and=C2=A0the banks limit the amount of=
 PIK debt they can do (because it=E2=80=99s risky), so this is a way around=
 that. I wrote:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The private cre=
dit fund doesn=E2=80=99t care: Paying interest in the form of more debt, or=
 paying interest in cash while simultaneously borrowing more, are exactly i=
dentical transactions as far as its credit risk is concerned. What matters =
is that the private credit fund has some contract with somebody else that s=
ays =E2=80=9Cwe promise not to do too many PIK deals,=E2=80=9D and a synthe=
tic PIK deal is not <em>technically </em>a PIK deal. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The som=
ebody else, specifically, is the banks.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">Well, a=C2=A0reader emailed to point out that there=E2=80=99s ano=
ther somebody else:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Most privat=
e credit funds collect management fees based on invested LP [<em>limited pa=
rtner</em>]=C2=A0capital. Traditionally, if an investment was accruing PIK =
interest, they=E2=80=99d only get management fees paid on the initial princ=
ipal amount funded with LP capital and not the accreted PIK portion. Now th=
at they are funding it with DDTL [<em>a=C2=A0</em><em>delayed draw term loa=
n</em>], they get to collect a management fee on that piece that is funded =
to pay the interest. So, the LPs are paying a management fee for as long as=
 the DDTL drawn loan is outstanding (perhaps a couple of years?) to pay the=
mselves one quarter=E2=80=99s worth of interest and are concurrently increa=
sing their invested capital exposure to that specific deal.</p></blockquote=
><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If a private credit fund=C2=A0does a $100 PIK=
 loan with 10% interest, after a year the borrower owes $110 and the fund c=
an charge fees on=C2=A0$100 of capital. If it does the same loan as a synth=
etic PIK, after a year=C2=A0the borrower owes $110 and the fund can charge =
fees on=C2=A0$110 of capital.=C2=A0</p><table class=3D"header" width=3D"100=
